input.prompt:
In a .NET 11 library I'm working on, I have a `JsonSerializerOptions` instance
and I want to get the JSON metadata for a specific type `T` in a strongly-typed
way (i.e. I want back a `JsonTypeInfo<T>`, not a non-generic `JsonTypeInfo` that
I have to cast).

I also need a way to *probe* whether metadata for `T` is available without
having an exception thrown at me if it isn't â€” so I can branch on "have it"
vs "don't have it". I'd rather not wrap things in try/catch.

Show me a minimal `net11.0` program that demonstrates both: getting the typed
metadata when it's available, and checking-without-throwing for a case where
it might not be. Run the program.

newMessages.content:
<skill-context name="system-text-json-net11">
Base directory for this skill: /tmp/sv-iso-3d48703bbd224666ac77e74e474365f4/system-text-json-net11

---
name: system-text-json-net11
description: >
  Provides guidance on new System.Text.Json APIs introduced in .NET 11.
  It covers typed JsonTypeInfo access via GetTypeInfo<T> and TryGetTypeInfo<T> on
  JsonSerializerOptions, and the new JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase static property.
  Use when serializing or deserializing JSON in .NET 11 applications and needing
  typed metadata access or PascalCase property naming.
license: MIT
---

# System.Text.Json â€” .NET 11

New APIs added to `System.Text.Json` across .NET 11 releases.

## When to Use

- Serializing or deserializing JSON in a .NET 11 (or later) project
- Needing strongly-typed `JsonTypeInfo<T>` access instead of the untyped `JsonTypeInfo` overload
- Wanting to safely check whether type metadata is available without catching exceptions (`TryGetTypeInfo<T>`)
- Requiring PascalCase property naming during JSON serialization

## When Not to Use

- The project targets .NET 10 or earlier â€” these APIs are not available before .NET 11
- Using a JSON library that is not `System.Text.Json` (e.g., Newtonsoft.Json)
- The existing untyped `GetTypeInfo(Type)` / `TryGetTypeInfo(Type, ...)` overloads are sufficient

## Target Framework

```xml
<TargetFramework>net11.0</TargetFramework>
```

## New APIs

### Typed `JsonTypeInfo` Access

#### `JsonSerializerOptions.GetTypeInfo<T>()`

Returns a strongly-typed `JsonTypeInfo<T>` for the specified type, using the
options' configured type-info resolver.

```csharp
JsonTypeInfo<T> GetTypeInfo<T>()
```

#### `JsonSerializerOptions.TryGetTypeInfo<T>(out JsonTypeInfo<T>?)`

Attempts to retrieve typed metadata without throwing if the type is not resolved.

```csharp
bool TryGetTypeInfo<T>(out JsonTypeInfo<T>? typeInfo)
```

### `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase`

A new static property that converts property names to PascalCase during
serialization.

```csharp
static JsonNamingPolicy PascalCase { get; }
```

## Examples

### Get Typed JsonTypeInfo

```csharp
using System.Text.Json;
using System.Text.Json.Serialization.Metadata;

var options = new JsonSerializerOptions(JsonSerializerDefaults.Web);

// Retrieve strongly-typed metadata for MyClass
JsonTypeInfo<MyClass> typeInfo = options.GetTypeInfo<MyClass>();
Console.WriteLine($"Type: {typeInfo.Type.Name}");
```

### TryGetTypeInfo for Safe Access

```csharp
using System.Text.Json;
using System.Text.Json.Serialization.Metadata;

var options = new JsonSerializerOptions(JsonSerializerDefaults.Web);

if (options.TryGetTypeInfo<MyClass>(out var info))
{
    Console.WriteLine($"Resolved type info for {info!.Type.Name}");
}
else
{
    Console.WriteLine("Type info not available");
}
```

### PascalCase Naming Policy

```csharp
using System.Text.Json;

var opts = new JsonSerializerOptions
{
    PropertyNamingPolicy = JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase
};

var obj = new { firstName = "John", lastName = "Doe" };
string json = JsonSerializer.Serialize(obj, opts);
Console.WriteLine(json);
// Output: {"FirstName":"John","LastName":"Doe"}
```

### Combined: Serialize with Typed Metadata

```csharp
using System.Text.Json;
using System.Text.Json.Serialization.Metadata;

var options = new JsonSerializerOptions
{
    PropertyNamingPolicy = JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase
};

JsonTypeInfo<Person> typeInfo = options.GetTypeInfo<Person>();
string json = JsonSerializer.Serialize(new Person("Jane", 30), typeInfo);
Console.WriteLine(json);
// Output: {"Name":"Jane","Age":30}

public record Person(string Name, int Age);
```

</skill-context>

data.reasoningText:
The default `JsonSerializerOptions` doesn't have a TypeInfoResolver set. I need to set the default resolver. Let me fix this - the `JsonSerializerDefaults.Web` preset doesn't include a type info resolver. I need to explicitly set one.

Let me check - in .NET 8+, `JsonSerializerOptions` requires a `TypeInfoResolver`. The default options should have `DefaultJsonTypeInfoResolver` but it seems it doesn't here. Let me adjust the code.

data.content: The source-gen context auto-includes `int` because `Person.Age` is an `int`. Let me pick a type that truly isn't in the context.
data.reasoningText:
Hmm, the `TryGetTypeInfo<int>` succeeded because the reflection-based resolver also resolves `int`. But I want to demonstrate the case where it returns false. Let me adjust - I should use the source-gen context (AppJsonContext) that only knows about `Person`, so `int` would fail. Let me check the output again - the last line says "TryGet succeeded for Int32" which is wrong for my scenario. Wait, looking at the code again:

```csharp
var limited = new JsonSerializerOptions { TypeInfoResolver = AppJsonContext.Default };
```

This uses the source-gen context. 

Looking at the actual output, I'm seeing three lines - the serialized Person object, then TryGet succeeding for Person, and then TryGet also succeeding for Int32. That's unexpected since the source-gen context should only know about Person. It seems like the source-gen context might be falling back to reflection or something else is going on.

Since Person has an `int Age` property, the source generator probably included `int` in its resolver. I should test with a type that's definitely not part of the source-gen context, like `Dictionary<string, object>` or something unrelated, to see if that gets rejected properly.

data.content: All three cases work. Here's the final program:

**Two new .NET 11 APIs demonstrated:**

| API | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `options.GetTypeInfo<T>()` | Returns `JsonTypeInfo<T>` directly â€” no cast from the non-generic overload |
| `options.TryGetTypeInfo<T>(out â€¦)` | Returns `false` instead of throwing when the resolver can't handle `T` |

The key trick for the `TryGetTypeInfo` demo: we use a **source-gen context** (`AppJsonContext`) that only knows about `Person`. Asking it for `Uri` returns `false` gracefully, whereas `GetTypeInfo<Uri>()` would throw `NotSupportedException`.
